questions.question: What turns an ordinary cache miss into a cache stampede?
options: Many concurrent callers missing the same hot key and independently refilling it.
A single request hitting a cold key at low traffic.
A cache node using compression.
Any read that bypasses the browser cache.
questions.explanation: Stampedes are about synchronized miss fanout, not just about a miss existing.

questions.question: Why is request coalescing valuable?
options: It lets one in-flight refresh serve many waiters instead of making the origin do repeated duplicate work.
It guarantees every request returns instantly.
It eliminates the need for cache keys.
It turns stale data into strongly consistent data.
questions.explanation: Coalescing shrinks miss amplification on hot keys.

questions.question: What problem does TTL jitter address?
options: It reduces synchronized expirations that would otherwise create periodic refill spikes.
It makes every key permanent.
It removes the need for origin capacity planning.
It guarantees cache hits on every request.
questions.explanation: Jitter spreads expiry over time so many keys do not refresh together.

questions.question: When is stale-while-revalidate especially attractive?
options: When bounded staleness is safer than blocking callers or flooding the origin during refresh.
When strict no-stale reads are required for every field.
When the cache is only used for writes.
When the source of truth has already been decommissioned.
questions.explanation: Slight staleness can be a good trade when the alternative is overload or latency collapse.

questions.question: Which metric best detects a developing stampede problem?
options: Origin fetches per hot key or per cache miss, especially right after expiry.
Only total company headcount.
The number of cache policy diagrams.
Average response compression ratio.
questions.explanation: Miss amplification shows whether each expired object is causing too many backend fetches.

questions.question: What is the hidden trade-off of request coalescing?
options: It lowers origin load but adds coordination overhead and waiter management on the miss path.
It strengthens durability at no cost.
It eliminates invalidation design.
It guarantees the origin will never fail.
questions.explanation: Coalescing is usually worth it for hot keys, but it is not free to implement.

questions.question: Which interview statement is strongest?
options: A high hit ratio means stampedes are not worth discussing.
I would explain steady-state hits separately from the hot-key miss path, then add coalescing, jitter, and possibly stale serving to protect the origin.
I would lower TTL until all keys refresh constantly.
I would solve stampedes by making every read a database read.
questions.explanation: Strong answers explicitly separate steady-state cache value from bursty refill failure modes.

questions.question: What if stale data is unacceptable for one endpoint under stampede conditions?
options: Use selective strictness there and discuss whether blocking, limited queueing, or load shedding is safer than flooding the origin.
Disable all caches globally.
Pretend stampede risk no longer applies.
Increase only the browser cache TTL.
questions.explanation: Critical endpoints may need stricter behavior, but the alternative miss-path control still must be designed.
